standard_description:
Explain why a fraction a/b is equivalent to a fraction (n ├Ś a)/(n ├Ś b) by using visual fraction models, with attention to how the number and size of the parts differ even though the two fractions themselves are the same size. Use this principle to recognize and generate equivalent fractions.
Grade 4 expectations in this domain are limited to fractions with denominators 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, and 100.
subject: Math

$${{{{2\over3}}}}$$

$${{{1\over3}}}+{{{1\over3}}}$$

Students usually first see fraction equivalence by subdividing the pieces into smaller,
equal sized pieces and realizing that you haven't changed the amount that represents
the fraction. This explains why if you multiply the numerator and denominator by the
same whole number, you get an equivalent fraction:
This task helps set students on the path to understanding that if you divide the
numerator and denominator by the same whole number, you get an equivalent
fraction. It is as if we are "putting smaller pieces together to form bigger pieces," as
opposed to subdividing the pieces as seen in, e.g. 4.NF Explaining Fraction Equivalence
with Pictures.
Students don't necessarily generate representations like the one shown in the task
stem where the pieces are "scattered around," so it is good for them to see and think
about such representations.

items.question_content:
**Compare using $>$, $<$, or $=$.**

  $\dfrac{7}{12}$ [[Ōśā dropdown 1]] $\dfrac{1}{2}$
hints: Let's rewrite the fractions to have a common denominator of $12$.

Let's split  $\dfrac{1}{2}$ into twelfths. 



[[Ōśā image 1]]


[[Ōśā image 2]]

$\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}} = \maroonD{\dfrac{6}{12}}$

Now that our fractions have the same denominator, we can compare. 



[[Ōśā image 1]]

Which fraction has the largest shaded area?
$\blueD{\dfrac{7}{12}} > \maroonD{\dfrac{6}{12}}$
$\blueD{\dfrac{7}{12}} > \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}}$

khan_exercises.title: Equivalent fractions
khan_exercises.description: Practice making equivalent fractions by relating their denominators.

items.question_content:
**What number could replace $t$ below?**

 $\dfrac{1}{3} = \dfrac{t}{6}$

$t=$ [[Ōśā numeric-input 1]]
hints:
Let's represent $\dfrac{1}{3}$ using a visual model.

![rectangle with 1 of 3 equal parts shaded, with each part labeled 1 third](web+graphie:${ŌśŻ LOCALPATH}/images/d7cb4dd833f8e80ce08a7b0e58380717ea59a2d3)

How many sixths will we need to shade to have the same amount shaded as $\dfrac{1}{3}$?

![rectangle with 1of 3 equal parts shaded, with each part labeled 1 third. same sized rectangle below it, with 6 equal parts, each labeled with 1 sixth](web+graphie:${ŌśŻ LOCALPATH}/images/aaae56285ae349c2776683550ed69ff1aab8842d)

We need to shade $\greenD2$ sixths.

![rectangle with 1of 3 equal parts shaded, with each part labeled 1 third. same sized rectangle below it, with 2 of 6 equal parts shaded, each labeled with 1 sixth](web+graphie:${ŌśŻ LOCALPATH}/images/4f69e5742ecd7118bf2fa8fd408449575d4fb200)

 $\dfrac{1}{3} = \dfrac{\greenD2}{6}$
$\greenD{t}=\greenD{2}$

items.question_content:
**What number could replace $m$ below?**

 $\dfrac{m}{8} = \dfrac{1}{2}$

$m=$ [[Ōśā numeric-input 1]]
hints:
Let's represent $\dfrac{1}{2}$ using a visual model.

![rectangle with 1 of 2 equal parts shaded, with each part labeled 1 half](web+graphie:${ŌśŻ LOCALPATH}/images/46d5290e176c7614b397ecda983c0eaacc001d78)

How many eighths will we need to shade to have the same amount shaded as $\dfrac{1}{2}$?

![rectangle with 1of 2 equal parts shaded, with each part labeled 1 half. same sized rectangle below it, with 8 equal parts, each labeled with 1 eighth](web+graphie:${ŌśŻ LOCALPATH}/images/eab22ef7a1edd06a3b2aa316642ea117ce701c94)

We need to shade $\greenD4$ eighths.

![rectangle with 1 of 2 equal parts shaded, with each part labeled 1 half. same sized rectangle below it, with 4 of 8 equal parts shaded, each labeled with 1 eighth](web+graphie:${ŌśŻ LOCALPATH}/images/31dd66b88d20c30aaadc4448f7ebd9728131e0f1)

 $\dfrac{\greenD4}{8} = \dfrac{1}{2}$
$\greenD{m}=\greenD{4}$

items.question_content:
**What number could replace $p$ below?**

 $\dfrac{p}{4} = \dfrac{6}{8}$

$p=$ [[Ōśā numeric-input 1]]
hints:
Let's represent $\dfrac{6}{8}$ using a visual model.

![rectangle with 6 of 8 equal parts shaded, with each part labeled 1 eighth](web+graphie:${ŌśŻ LOCALPATH}/images/cd23df65b1e4af77c8942458c19802d7cce786be)

How many fourths will we need to shade to have the same amount shaded as $\dfrac{6}{8}$?

![rectangle with 6 of 8 equal parts shaded, with each part labeled 1 eighth. same sized rectangle below it, with 4 equal parts, each labeled with 1 fourth](web+graphie:${ŌśŻ LOCALPATH}/images/7e80871c564b814eb7b78351eef77bc62e3cd8b8)

We need to shade $\greenD3$ fourths.

![rectangle with 6 of 8 equal parts shaded, with each part labeled 1 eighth. same sized rectangle below it, with 3 of 4 equal parts shaded, each labeled with 1 fourth](web+graphie:${ŌśŻ LOCALPATH}/images/5df0da1b0d9bf292e9f082ac0357c8b9304834d3)

 $\dfrac{\greenD3}{4} = \dfrac{6}{8}$
$\greenD{p}=\greenD{3}$

items.question_content:
**What value of $k$ makes the equation true?**

 $\dfrac{4}{k} = \dfrac{2}{3}$

$k=$ [[Ōśā numeric-input 1]]
hints: Equivalent fractions cover the same area of the whole.

Let's represent $\dfrac{2}{3}$ using a visual model.

![rectangle with 2 of 3 equal parts shaded, with each part labeled 1 third.](web+graphie:${ŌśŻ LOCALPATH}/images/f4c867d5e2196c4febd679e4b81ff39e677aeeb4)

We need $4$ parts in the equivalent fraction to cover the same area as $\dfrac{2}{\blueD{3}}$.

![rectangle with 2 of 3 equal parts shaded, with each part labeled 1 third.](web+graphie:${ŌśŻ LOCALPATH}/images/f4c867d5e2196c4febd679e4b81ff39e677aeeb4)  
![same-sized rectangle with 4 of an unknown number of equal parts shaded, each labeled 1 over unknown number.](web+graphie:${ŌśŻ LOCALPATH}/images/abf8c5b8eb780336f389488ffd3fed76c6538020)

How many equal parts should the equivalent fraction have?

We need $\maroonD{6}$ equal parts per whole.

![rectangle with 2 of 3 equal parts shaded, with each part labeled 1 third.](web+graphie:${ŌśŻ LOCALPATH}/images/f4c867d5e2196c4febd679e4b81ff39e677aeeb4)  
![same-sized rectangle with 2 of 6 equal parts shaded, with each part labeled 1 sixth.](web+graphie:${ŌśŻ LOCALPATH}/images/3455bd7cf49f4f99e66f7ea1e1ecc2a92ec44621)

 $\dfrac{4}{\maroonD{6}} = \dfrac{2}{3}$
$\maroonD{k}=\maroonD{6}$

items.question_content:
**What value of $w$ makes the equation true?**

 $\dfrac{1}{4} = \dfrac{2}{w}$

$w=$ [[Ōśā numeric-input 1]]
hints: Equivalent fractions cover the same area of the whole.

Let's represent $\dfrac{1}{4}$ using a visual model.

![rectangle with 1 of 4 equal parts shaded, with each part labeled 1 fourth.](web+graphie:${ŌśŻ LOCALPATH}/images/f7ddf8c939d7e89178d1e60687e729b8c982e151)

We need $2$ parts in the equivalent fraction to cover the same area as $\dfrac{1}{\blueD{4}}$.

![rectangle with 1 of 4 equal parts shaded, with each part labeled 1 fourth.](web+graphie:${ŌśŻ LOCALPATH}/images/f7ddf8c939d7e89178d1e60687e729b8c982e151)  
![same-sized rectangle with 2 of an unknown number of equal parts shaded, each labeled 1 over unknown number.](web+graphie:${ŌśŻ LOCALPATH}/images/0e959a9cca19c8808f5b3a520a073b9a58821888)

How many equal parts should the equivalent fraction have?

We need $\maroonD{8}$ equal parts per whole.

![rectangle with 1 of 4 equal parts shaded, with each part labeled 1 fourth.](web+graphie:${ŌśŻ LOCALPATH}/images/f7ddf8c939d7e89178d1e60687e729b8c982e151)  
![same-sized rectangle with 2 of 8 equal parts shaded, with each part labeled 1 eighth.](web+graphie:${ŌśŻ LOCALPATH}/images/36ca9771d8eede22007715f1e2af81d0a0ba97b8)

$\dfrac{1}{4} = \dfrac{2}{\maroonD{8}}$
$\maroonD{w}=\maroonD{8}$

items.question_content:
**What value of $c$ makes the equation true?**

 $\dfrac{3}{6} = \dfrac{4}{c}$

$c=$ [[Ōśā numeric-input 1]]
hints: Equivalent fractions cover the same area of the whole.

Let's represent $\dfrac{3}{6}$ using a visual model.

![rectangle with 3 of 6 equal parts shaded, with each part labeled 1 sixth.](web+graphie:${ŌśŻ LOCALPATH}/images/cdea38c22eaf93cee9be36e4cef6a4edeaf17d80)

We need $4$ parts in the equivalent fraction to cover the same area as $\dfrac{3}{\blueD{6}}$.

![rectangle with 3 of 6 equal parts shaded, with each part labeled 1 sixth.](web+graphie:${ŌśŻ LOCALPATH}/images/cdea38c22eaf93cee9be36e4cef6a4edeaf17d80)
![same-sized rectangle with 4 of an unknown number of equal parts shaded, each labeled 1 over unknown number.](web+graphie:${ŌśŻ LOCALPATH}/images/d6b7e0533c80adc575dc9ed52b57c0a931dcb52c)

How many equal parts should the equivalent fraction have?

We need $\maroonD{8}$ equal parts per whole.

![rectangle with 3 of 6 equal parts shaded, with each part labeled 1 sixth.](web+graphie:${ŌśŻ LOCALPATH}/images/cdea38c22eaf93cee9be36e4cef6a4edeaf17d80) 
![same-sized rectangle with 4 of 8 equal parts shaded, with each part labeled 1 eighth.](web+graphie:${ŌśŻ LOCALPATH}/images/470468218d6701200fd8166e407053f307f62445)

$\dfrac{3}{6} = \dfrac{4}{\maroonD{8}}$
$\maroonD{c}=\maroonD{8}$

items.question_content:
**Which of the following fractions are equivalent to $\dfrac24$?**


[[Ōśā radio 1]]
hints: For fractions to be equivalent, they must represent the same area of the whole.
Let's use a visual model to see which fractions shade the same area of the whole as $\blueD{\dfrac{2}{4}}$.

![rectangle with 2 of 4 equal parts shaded, with each part labeled 1 fourth. same sized rectangle below it, with 1 of 2 equal parts shaded, each labeled with 1 half](web+graphie:${ŌśŻ LOCALPATH}/images/c9b301115246e979221febdca75032a7b44522a5)

$\blueD{\dfrac{2}{4}} = \greenD{\dfrac{1}{2}}$

![rectangle with 1 of 4 equal parts shaded, with each part labeled 1 fourth. same sized rectangle below it, with 2 of 6 equal parts shaded, each labeled with 1 sixth](web+graphie:${ŌśŻ LOCALPATH}/images/de25b3d6e8b8141e161448f996bbfb78be9a548a)

$\blueD{\dfrac{2}{4}} \neq \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{6}}$

![rectangle with 2 of 4 equal parts shaded, with each part labeled 1 fourth. same sized rectangle below it, with 4 of 8 equal parts shaded, each labeled with 1 eighth](web+graphie:${ŌśŻ LOCALPATH}/images/1ac6f3a25f883bb266efcf1f30671229a7427a1d)

$\blueD{\dfrac{2}{4}} = \purpleD{\dfrac{4}{8}}$

items.question_content:
**Which number line shows a number equivalent to $\dfrac{2}{6}$? **

[[Ōśā radio 2]]
hints:
Numbers are equivalent when they represent the same place on the number line.

Let's compare each point to $\blueD{\dfrac26}$ and find the equivalent fraction.

$\greenD{M}$ is located at $\greenD{\dfrac14}$.



[[Ōśā image 1]]

$\greenD{\dfrac14} \neq \blueD{\dfrac26}$

$\purpleD{N}$ is located at $\purpleD{\dfrac48}$.



[[Ōśā image 1]]

$\purpleD{\dfrac48} \neq \blueD{\dfrac26}$

$\maroonD{L}$ is located at $\maroonD{\dfrac13}$.



[[Ōśā image 1]]

$\maroonD{\dfrac13} = \blueD{\dfrac26}$
$\maroonD{L}$ is in the same place on the number line as $\blueD{\dfrac26}$.

The following number line is equivalent to $\dfrac{2}{6}$.



[[Ōśā image 1]]

items.question_content:
**Which number line shows a number equivalent to $\dfrac{2}{4}$? **

[[Ōśā radio 2]]
hints:
Numbers are equivalent when they represent the same place on the number line.

Let's compare each point to $\blueD{\dfrac24}$ and find the equivalent fraction.

$\maroonD{L}$ is located at $\maroonD{\dfrac23}$.



[[Ōśā image 1]]

$\maroonD{\dfrac23} \neq \blueD{\dfrac24}$

$\greenD{M}$ is located at $\greenD{\dfrac26}$.



[[Ōśā image 1]]

$\greenD{\dfrac26} \neq \blueD{\dfrac24}$

$\purpleD{N}$ is located at $\purpleD{\dfrac48}$.



[[Ōśā image 1]]

$\purpleD{\dfrac48} = \blueD{\dfrac24}$
$\purpleD{N}$ is located at the same point on the number line as $\blueD{\dfrac24}$.

The following number line is equivalent to $\dfrac{2}{4}$.



[[Ōśā image 1]]
